children.value: ##1.3 Gaussian Elimination to Upper Triangular Form
As an alternative to using Gaussian Elimination to obtain the Row Reduced Echelon Form of the augmented system, we could have stopped at the
, that is the matrix that has entries

children.html: aij=0a_{ij}=0aij​=0
i>ji>ji>j

children.value: .  That is, all the entries below the main diagonal are zero.

children.html: [123∣4015∣100−22∣0]\begin{bmatrix} 1&2&3&|&4\\ 0&1&5&|&1\\ 0&0&-22&|&0 \end{bmatrix}⎣⎡​100​210​35−22​∣∣∣​410​⎦⎤​

children.value: If a matrix is in upper triangular form, then backward substitution can be used to finish the solution.  That is solve the bottom equation for one variable, then substitute that in to the previous equation to solve for the next variable and repeat moving backwards until done.

children.html: 22x3=0→x3=022x_3=0\rightarrow x_3=022x3​=0→x3​=0
x2+5x3=1,x_2+5x_3=1,x2​+5x3​=1,
x3=0x_3=0x3​=0
x2+0=1→x2=1x_2+0=1\rightarrow x_2=1x2​+0=1→x2​=1
x1+2x2+3x3=4x_1+2x_2+3x_3=4x1​+2x2​+3x3​=4
x2=1 x3=0x_2=1~x_3=0x2​=1 x3​=0
x1+2+0=4→x1=2x_1+2+0=4\rightarrow x_1=2x1​+2+0=4→x1​=2
Ax=bA\mathbf{x}=\mathbf{b}Ax=b

children.value: by writing it as the augmented matrix

children.html: [A∣b][A|\mathbf{b}][A∣b]

children.value: and then using gaussian elimination until we arrive at the upper triangular matrix

children.html: [U∣c][U|\mathbf{c}][U∣c]

children.value: .  Then backwards substitution can be used to find the solution.
Below is code that works for generic regular matrices.
are square matrices where the regular Gaussian elimination algorithm reduces to an upper triangular matrix with no zeros on the main diagonal.  We will worry about non-regular matrices later.

In general, for the regular case, to get an upper triangular matrix, we just use the entry in the first row and first column to zero out everything below it.  Then use the new entry in the second row and second column to zero out everything below it, and so on until done.

children.html: ajj=0a_{jj}=0ajj​=0
AAA

children.html: Eij(−aij/aii)E_{ij}(-a_{ij}/a_{ii})Eij​(−aij​/aii​)

children.value: Backwards substitution can be done in general as well.

children.html: xn=cn/unnx_n=c_n/u_{nn}xn​=cn​/unn​
xi=1uii(ci−∑j=i+1nuijxj) x_i=\dfrac{1}{u_{ii}}\left(c_i-\sum_{j=i+1}^{n}u_{ij}x_j \right)xi​=uii​1​(ci​−∑j=i+1n​uij​xj​)

children.value: See below for Python code performing this on arbitrary square matrices.

from numpy.random import seed, choice, shuffle
from numpy import sum
from sympy import Matrix, latex, solve, zeros
from sympy.abc import x, y, z
from IPython.display import display, Math
from ipywidgets import interact
import ipywidgets as widgets

def f(seednum,n):

  P=range(1,10)
  seed(seednum)
  Aug = Matrix(choice(P, (n, n+1)))


  A, b = Aug[:, :n], Aug[:, n]


  display(Math(f'{latex(Aug[:,:n])}\\mathbf{{x}}={latex(Aug[:,n])}'))
  #sol = Matrix([[x], [y], [z]])
  #display(Math(f'{latex(A)}{latex(sol)}={latex(b)}'))
  #print(solve(A*sol - b, [x, y, z]))

#Gaussian Elimination
  for j in range(0,n): #keep in mind we start in the 0th row and 0th column, this does j=0. j=1 and j=2
    if Aug[j,j] == 0:
      print("Matrix is not regular, try a different seed")
      break
    else:
      for i in range(j+1,n):
        Aug[i,:]=Aug[i,:]-(Aug[i,j]/Aug[j,j])*Aug[j,:]


  display(Math(f'{latex(Aug[:,:n])}\\mathbf{{x}}={latex(Aug[:,n])}'))

  sol=zeros(n,1)

#Backward Substituion
  sol[n-1]=Aug[n-1,n]/Aug[n-1,n-1] #x_n=c_n/u_{nn}
  for i in range(n-2,-1,-1):
    sol[i]=Aug[i,n]
    for j in range(i+1,n):
      sol[i]=sol[i]-(Aug[i,j]*sol[j])
    sol[i]=sol[i]/Aug[i,i]

  print("Backwards Solution")
  display(Math(f'{latex(sol)}'))
  print("Check")
  display(Math(f'{latex(A)}{latex(sol)}={latex(A*sol)}={latex(b)}'))


interact(f,
         seednum=widgets.IntText(value=1234, description='Seed='),
         n=widgets.IntText(value=3, description='Number of Variables='),
          );

children.value: is chosen to be lower unitriangular, meaning it is lower triangular and the main diagonal are all 1s.  The standard method is to use standard Gaussian elimination until the result is upper triangular.  For example
Consider the set of linear equations

children.html: [123231−21−3]\begin{bmatrix} 1&2&3\\ 2&3&1\\ -2&1&-3 \end{bmatrix}⎣⎡​12−2​231​31−3​⎦⎤​

children.value: Step 1) Multiply by the Elementary Matrix

children.html: E21(−2)E_{21}(-2)E21​(−2)
[1230−1−5−21−3]\begin{bmatrix} 1&2&3\\ 0&-1&-5\\ -2&1&-3 \end{bmatrix}⎣⎡​10−2​2−11​3−5−3​⎦⎤​

children.value: Step 2) Multiply by the Elementary Matrix

children.html: E31(2)E_{31}(2)E31​(2)
[1230−1−5053]\begin{bmatrix} 1&2&3\\ 0&-1&-5\\ 0&5&3 \end{bmatrix}⎣⎡​100​2−15​3−53​⎦⎤​

children.value: Step 3) Multiply by the Elementary Matrix

children.html: E32(5)E_{32}(5)E32​(5)
[1230−1−500−22]\begin{bmatrix} 1&2&3\\ 0&-1&-5\\ 0&0&-22 \end{bmatrix}⎣⎡​100​2−10​3−5−22​⎦⎤​

children.value: Notice, we did not bother making the diagonal elements 1, we just made it upper triangular.
From what we learned about elementary matrices we know this means.

children.html: E32(5)E31(2)E21(−2)[123231−21−3]=[1230−1−500−22]E_{32}(5)E_{31}(2)E_{21}(-2)\begin{bmatrix} 1&2&3\\ 2&3&1\\ -2&1&-3 \end{bmatrix}=\begin{bmatrix} 1&2&3\\ 0&-1&-5\\ 0&0&-22 \end{bmatrix}E32​(5)E31​(2)E21​(−2)⎣⎡​12−2​231​31−3​⎦⎤​=⎣⎡​100​2−10​3−5−22​⎦⎤​

E32(5)E31(2)E21(−2)=[100010051][100010201][100−210001]=[100−210−851]E_{32}(5)E_{31}(2)E_{21}(-2)=\begin{bmatrix} 1&0&0\\ 0&1&0\\ 0&5&1\end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1&0&0\\ 0&1&0\\ 2&0&1\end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix} 1&0&0\\ -2&1&0\\ 0&0&1\end{bmatrix}=\begin{bmatrix} 1&0&0\\ -2&1&0\\ -8&5&1\end{bmatrix}E32​(5)E31​(2)E21​(−2)=⎣⎡​100​015​001​⎦⎤​⎣⎡​102​010​001​⎦⎤​⎣⎡​1−20​010​001​⎦⎤​=⎣⎡​1−2−8​015​001​⎦⎤​

children.value: The pivot operations to obtain an upper triangular matrix were all operations that add or subtract from the lower triangular, so their product of the elementary matrices is upper triangular.  Because we did not try to get 1s on the main diagonal earlier none of the operations we did multiplied any of the rows by constants so the product of the elementary row matrices will have just ones on the diagonal.

children.html: [100−210−851]A=[1230−1−500−22]\begin{bmatrix} 1&0&0\\ -2&1&0\\ -8&5&1\end{bmatrix}A= \begin{bmatrix} 1&2&3\\ 0&-1&-5\\ 0&0&-22 \end{bmatrix}⎣⎡​1−2−8​015​001​⎦⎤​A=⎣⎡​100​2−10​3−5−22​⎦⎤​

A=[100−210−851]−1[1230−1−500−22]=[100210−2−51][1230−1−500−22]=A= \begin{bmatrix} 1&0&0\\ -2&1&0\\ -8&5&1\end{bmatrix}^{-1}\begin{bmatrix} 1&2&3\\ 0&-1&-5\\ 0&0&-22 \end{bmatrix}=\begin{bmatrix} 1&0&0\\ 2&1&0\\ -2&-5&1\end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix} 1&2&3\\ 0&-1&-5\\ 0&0&-22 \end{bmatrix}=A=⎣⎡​1−2−8​015​001​⎦⎤​−1⎣⎡​100​2−10​3−5−22​⎦⎤​=⎣⎡​12−2​01−5​001​⎦⎤​⎣⎡​100​2−10​3−5−22​⎦⎤​=

children.value: Notice that this inverse of the lower triangular matrix is lower triangular.  That is because

children.html: (E32(5)E31(2)E21(−2))−1=([100010051][100010201][100−210001])−1(E_{32}(5)E_{31}(2)E_{21}(-2))^{-1}=\left(\begin{bmatrix} 1&0&0\\ 0&1&0\\ 0&5&1\end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1&0&0\\ 0&1&0\\ 2&0&1\end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix} 1&0&0\\ -2&1&0\\ 0&0&1\end{bmatrix}\right)^{-1}(E32​(5)E31​(2)E21​(−2))−1=⎝⎛​⎣⎡​100​015​001​⎦⎤​⎣⎡​102​010​001​⎦⎤​⎣⎡​1−20​010​001​⎦⎤​⎠⎞​−1

E21(+2)E31(−2)E32(−5)=[100210001][100010−201][1000100−51]=[100210−2−51]E_{21}(+2)E_{31}(-2)E_{32}(-5)=\begin{bmatrix} 1&0&0\\ 2&1&0\\ 0&0&1\end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix} 1&0&0\\ 0&1&0\\ -2&0&1\end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix} 1&0&0\\ 0&1&0\\ 0&-5&1\end{bmatrix}=\begin{bmatrix} 1&0&0\\ 2&1&0\\ -2&-5&1\end{bmatrix}E21​(+2)E31​(−2)E32​(−5)=⎣⎡​120​010​001​⎦⎤​⎣⎡​10−2​010​001​⎦⎤​⎣⎡​100​01−5​001​⎦⎤​=⎣⎡​12−2​01−5​001​⎦⎤​

children.value: We are only doing the elementary row operations

children.html: Eij(c)E_{ij}(c)Eij​(c)
Eij(c)−1=Eij(−c)E_{ij}(c)^{-1}=E_{ij}(-c)Eij​(c)−1=Eij​(−c)

children.value: .  So, the inverse of the elementary row operations will still just be the product of elementary matrices that add or subtract from the lower triangular region, while leaving the main diagonal and upper triangular region untouched.

children.value: is the inverse of the product of the elementary matrices used.  If you look more carefully at

children.html: LLL

children.value: you can see it is just the product of the inverse operations.  In step 1) we subtracted 2 of the first row from the second row, the inverse of that is to add 2 of the first row to the second row, thus

children.html: L21=2L_{21}=2L21​=2

children.value: .  In step 2) we added 2 of the first row to the third row, so the inverse would be subtracting 2 of the first row from the third row, so

children.html: L31=−2L_{31}=-2L31​=−2

children.value: .  In step 3) we added 5 of the second row to the third row, so the inverse would be subtracting 5 of the second rom from the third row, thus

children.html: L32=−5L_{32}=-5L32​=−5
LLL

children.value: in the lower triangle are the negatives of multiples used to obtain the zeros in the upper triangular matrix.

from numpy.random import seed, choice, shuffle
from numpy import sum
from sympy import Matrix, latex, solve, zeros, eye
from sympy.abc import x, y, z
from IPython.display import display, Math
from ipywidgets import interact
import ipywidgets as widgets

def f(seednum,n):

  P=range(1,10)
  seed(seednum)
  Aug = Matrix(choice(P, (n, n+1)))


  A, b = Aug[:, :n], Aug[:, n]
  L=eye(n,n)
  U=Aug[:,:n]

  display(Math(f'{latex(Aug[:,:n])}\\mathbf{{x}}={latex(Aug[:,n])}'))
  #sol = Matrix([[x], [y], [z]])
  #display(Math(f'{latex(A)}{latex(sol)}={latex(b)}'))
  #print(solve(A*sol - b, [x, y, z]))

#Gaussian Elimination
  for j in range(0,n): #keep in mind we start in the 0th row and 0th column, this does j=0. j=1 and j=2
    if U[j,j] == 0:
      print("Matrix is not regular, try a different seed")
      break
    else:
      for i in range(j+1,n):
        L[i,j]=U[i,j]/U[j,j]
        U[i,:]=U[i,:]-(U[i,j]/U[j,j])*U[j,:]



  display(Math(f'U={latex(U)}'))

  display(Math(f'L={latex(L)}'))

  display(Math(f'A={latex(A)}={latex(L*U)}'))

  sol=zeros(n,1)

#Backward Substituion
  RHS=L**-1*b
  sol[n-1]=RHS[n-1]/U[n-1,n-1] #x_n=c_n/u_{nn}
  for i in range(n-2,-1,-1):
    sol[i]=RHS[i]
    for j in range(i+1,n):
      sol[i]=sol[i]-(U[i,j]*sol[j])
    sol[i]=sol[i]/U[i,i]

  print("Backwards Solution")
  display(Math(f'{latex(sol)}'))
  print("Check")
  display(Math(f'{latex(A)}{latex(sol)}={latex(A*sol)}={latex(b)}'))


interact(f,
         seednum=widgets.IntText(value=1234, description='Seed='),
         n=widgets.IntText(value=3, description='Number of Variables='),
          );

text/plain.content: interactive(children=(IntText(value=1234, description='Seed='), IntText(value=3, description='Number of Variab…

children.value: The regular Gaussian elimination works everytime for regular matrices, but it will give a divide by zero error if used for non-regular matrices.  To fix this we use a permutation matrix to transform

children.html: AAA

children.value: into a regular matrix.  For example let

children.html: A=[0123−12134]A=\begin{bmatrix}0&1&2\\3&-1&2\\1&3&4 \end{bmatrix}A=⎣⎡​031​1−13​224​⎦⎤​
AAA

children.value: is not regular, as there is a zero in the first row first column, so the step of

children.html: E21(−a21/a11)E_{21}(-a_{21}/a_{11})E21​(−a21​/a11​)

children.value: would fail.  But, if we just procede by first performing the elementary row operation

children.html: E21E_{21}E21​
E21A=[3−12012134]E_{21}A=\begin{bmatrix}3&-1&2\\0&1&2\\1&3&4 \end{bmatrix}E21​A=⎣⎡​301​−113​224​⎦⎤​

children.value: Step 1) Multiply by the Elementary Matrix

children.html: E31(−1/3)E_{31}(-1/3)E31​(−1/3)
E31(−1/3)E21A=[3−12012010/310/3]E_{31}(-1/3)E_{21}A=\begin{bmatrix}3&-1&2\\0&1&2\\0&10/3&10/3 \end{bmatrix}E31​(−1/3)E21​A=⎣⎡​300​−1110/3​2210/3​⎦⎤​

children.value: Step 2) Multiply by the Elementary Matrix

children.html: E32(−10/3)E_{32}(-10/3)E32​(−10/3)
E32(−10/3)E31(−1/3)E21A=[3−1201200−10/3]E_{32}(-10/3)E_{31}(-1/3)E_{21}A=\begin{bmatrix}3&-1&2\\0&1&2\\0&0&-10/3 \end{bmatrix}E32​(−10/3)E31​(−1/3)E21​A=⎣⎡​300​−110​22−10/3​⎦⎤​

children.value: Note the pivoting elementary row operations are not part of

children.html: LLL
UUU
PPP

children.value: to indicate the pivot matrix.  When a matrix is non-regular we find

children.html: PA=LUPA=LUPA=LU
AAA
PPP

children.value: is a pivot matrix, or product of pivot matrices,

children.html: LLL
UUU

children.value: is upper triangular.  For the previous example

children.html: A=[0123−12134]A=\begin{bmatrix}0&1&2\\3&-1&2\\1&3&4 \end{bmatrix}A=⎣⎡​031​1−13​224​⎦⎤​
P=[010100001]P=\begin{bmatrix}0&1&0\\1&0&0\\0&0&1 \end{bmatrix}P=⎣⎡​010​100​001​⎦⎤​
L=[1000101/310/31]L=\begin{bmatrix}1&0&0\\0&1&0\\1/3&10/3&1 \end{bmatrix}L=⎣⎡​101/3​0110/3​001​⎦⎤​
U=[3−1201200−10/3]U=\begin{bmatrix}3&-1&2\\0&1&2\\0&0&-10/3 \end{bmatrix}U=⎣⎡​300​−110​22−10/3​⎦⎤​

PA=[010100001][0123−12134]=[1000101/310/31][3−1201200−10/3]=LUPA=\begin{bmatrix}0&1&0\\1&0&0\\0&0&1 \end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix}0&1&2\\3&-1&2\\1&3&4 \end{bmatrix}=\begin{bmatrix}1&0&0\\0&1&0\\1/3&10/3&1 \end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix}3&-1&2\\0&1&2\\0&0&-10/3 \end{bmatrix}=LUPA=⎣⎡​010​100​001​⎦⎤​⎣⎡​031​1−13​224​⎦⎤​=⎣⎡​101/3​0110/3​001​⎦⎤​⎣⎡​300​−110​22−10/3​⎦⎤​=LU
PA=LUPA=LUPA=LU
A=P−1LUA=P^{-1}LUA=P−1LU
Eij−1=EijE_{ij}^{-1}=E_{ij}Eij−1​=Eij​

children.value: , that is the inverse of a single pivot operation is itself.  But,

children.html: (EijEjk)−1=EjkEij\left(E_{ij}E_{jk}\right)^{-1}=E_{jk}E_{ij}(Eij​Ejk​)−1=Ejk​Eij​

children.value: , that is when you do multiple pivots the inverse will be the same elementary matrices, but in the opposite order.

children.html: A=[0123−12134]=[010100001][1000101/310/31][3−1201200−10/3]=PLUA=\begin{bmatrix}0&1&2\\3&-1&2\\1&3&4 \end{bmatrix}=\begin{bmatrix}0&1&0\\1&0&0\\0&0&1 \end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix}1&0&0\\0&1&0\\1/3&10/3&1 \end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix}3&-1&2\\0&1&2\\0&0&-10/3 \end{bmatrix}=PLUA=⎣⎡​031​1−13​224​⎦⎤​=⎣⎡​010​100​001​⎦⎤​⎣⎡​101/3​0110/3​001​⎦⎤​⎣⎡​300​−110​22−10/3​⎦⎤​=PLU

children.value: Notice, this factorization is not unique.  Instead we could have chosen

children.html: P=[001100010]=[010100001][100001010]=E12E23P=\begin{bmatrix}0&0&1\\1&0&0\\0&1&0 \end{bmatrix}=\begin{bmatrix}0&1&0\\1&0&0\\0&0&1 \end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix}1&0&0\\0&0&1\\0&1&0 \end{bmatrix}=E_{12}E_{23}P=⎣⎡​010​001​100​⎦⎤​=⎣⎡​010​100​001​⎦⎤​⎣⎡​100​001​010​⎦⎤​=E12​E23​
E12E23A=[1340123−12]E_{12}E_{23}A=\begin{bmatrix}1&3&4\\0&1&2\\3&-1&2\\ \end{bmatrix}E12​E23​A=⎣⎡​103​31−1​422​⎦⎤​

children.value: Step 1) Multiply by the Elementary Matrix

children.html: E31(−3)E_{31}(-3)E31​(−3)
E31(−3)E12E23A=[1340120−10−10]E_{31}(-3)E_{12}E_{23}A=\begin{bmatrix}1&3&4\\0&1&2\\0&-10&-10 \end{bmatrix}E31​(−3)E12​E23​A=⎣⎡​100​31−10​42−10​⎦⎤​

children.value: Step 2) Multiply by the Elementary Matrix

children.html: E32(10)E_{32}(10)E32​(10)
E32(−10/3)E31(−1/3)E21A=[1340120010]E_{32}(-10/3)E_{31}(-1/3)E_{21}A=\begin{bmatrix}1&3&4\\0&1&2\\0&0&10 \end{bmatrix}E32​(−10/3)E31​(−1/3)E21​A=⎣⎡​100​310​4210​⎦⎤​
A=[0123−12134]A=\begin{bmatrix}0&1&2\\3&-1&2\\1&3&4 \end{bmatrix}A=⎣⎡​031​1−13​224​⎦⎤​
P=[001100010]P=\begin{bmatrix}0&0&1\\1&0&0\\0&1&0 \end{bmatrix}P=⎣⎡​010​001​100​⎦⎤​
L=[1000103−101]L=\begin{bmatrix}1&0&0\\0&1&0\\3&-10&1 \end{bmatrix}L=⎣⎡​103​01−10​001​⎦⎤​
U=[1340120010]U=\begin{bmatrix}1&3&4\\0&1&2\\0&0&10 \end{bmatrix}U=⎣⎡​100​310​4210​⎦⎤​

children.value: So, there can be multiple different PLU factorizations for the same matrix

children.html: AAA
PLUPLUPLU
UUU

children.value: having all non-zero elements on the diagonal, then

children.html: AAA
Ax=bA\mathbf{x}=\mathbf{b}Ax=b

children.value: has a unique solution for every choice of

children.html: b\mathbf{b}b
AAA

children.value: The more generic gaussian elimination allowing nonregular matrices can be written as

children.html: akj=0a_{kj}=0akj​=0
k≥jk\geq jk≥j
AAA
akj≠0a_{kj}\neq0akj​=0
k>jk>jk>j
EkjE_{kj}Ekj​
Eij(−aij/aii)E_{ij}(-a_{ij}/a_{ii})Eij​(−aij​/aii​)
Ax=bA\mathbf{x}=\mathbf{b}Ax=b
P−1LUx=bP^{-1}LU\mathbf{x}=\mathbf{b}P−1LUx=b
LUx=PbLU\mathbf{x}=P\mathbf{b}LUx=Pb

children.value: So, you can pivot the rows of

children.html: b\mathbf{b}b

children.value: and then use the last standard backwards substitution done earlier.

from numpy.random import seed, choice, shuffle
from numpy import sum
from sympy import Matrix, latex, solve, zeros, eye
from sympy.abc import x, y, z
from IPython.display import display, Math
from ipywidgets import interact
import ipywidgets as widgets

def f(seednum,n):

  P=range(0,5)
  seed(seednum)
  Aug = Matrix(choice(P, (n, n+1)))


  A, b = Aug[:, :n], Aug[:, n]
  P=eye(n,n)
  L=eye(n,n)
  U=Aug[:,:n]

  display(Math(f'{latex(Aug[:,:n])}\mathbf{{x}}={latex(Aug[:,n])}'))


#Gaussian Elimination
  for j in range(0,n): #keep in mind we start in the 0th row and 0th column, this does j=0. j=1 and j=2 if n=3
    if U[j,j] !=0:
      for i in range(j+1,n):
        L[i,j]=U[i,j]/U[j,j]
        U[i,:]=U[i,:]-(U[i,j]/U[j,j])*U[j,:]
    elif U[j,j] == 0:
      print("Matrix is not regular")
      singular=True
      for k in range(j,n):
        if U[k,j]!=0:
          P.row_swap(k,j)
          U.row_swap(k,j)
          for i in range(j+1,n):
            L[i,j]=U[i,j]/U[j,j]
            U[i,:]=U[i,:]-(U[i,j]/U[j,j])*U[j,:]
          temp=L[k,0:j]
          L[k,0:j]=L[j,0:j]
          L[j,0:j]=temp
          singular=False
          break
      if singular:
        print("Matrix is singular")
        break






  display(Math(f'P={latex(P)}'))

  display(Math(f'U={latex(U)}'))

  display(Math(f'L={latex(L)}'))

  display(Math(f'A={latex(A)}={latex(P.inv()*L*U)}'))

  sol=zeros(n,1)

#Backward Substitution
  RHS=L**-1*(P*b)
  sol[n-1]=RHS[n-1]/U[n-1,n-1] #x_n=c_n/u_{nn}
  for i in range(n-2,-1,-1):
    sol[i]=RHS[i]
    for j in range(i+1,n):
      sol[i]=sol[i]-(U[i,j]*sol[j])
    sol[i]=sol[i]/U[i,i]

  print("Backwards Solution")
  display(Math(f'{latex(sol)}'))
  print("Check")
  display(Math(f'{latex(A)}{latex(sol)}={latex(A*sol)}={latex(b)}'))


interact(f,
         seednum=widgets.IntText(value=1234, description='Seed='),
         n=widgets.IntText(value=3, description='Number of Variables='),
          );

U=[3−1201200−10/3]U=\begin{bmatrix}3&-1&2\\0&1&2\\0&0&-10/3 \end{bmatrix}U=⎣⎡​300​−110​22−10/3​⎦⎤​

How long does it take to solve a system?
Rather than determining time, which will vary from computer to computer or even on the same depending on how many background tasks are running, we focus on how many arithmetic operations are done.

from numpy.random import seed, choice, shuffle
from numpy import sum
from sympy import Matrix, latex, solve, zeros
from sympy.abc import x, y, z
from IPython.display import display, Math
from ipywidgets import interact
import ipywidgets as widgets
import time

def f(seednum,n):

  P=range(1,10)
  seed(seednum)
  Aug = Matrix(choice(P, (n, n+1)))


  A, b = Aug[:, :n], Aug[:, n]


  #display(Math(f'{latex(Aug[:,:n])}\mathbf{{x}}={latex(Aug[:,n])}'))
  #sol = Matrix([[x], [y], [z]])
  #display(Math(f'{latex(A)}{latex(sol)}={latex(b)}'))
  #print(solve(A*sol - b, [x, y, z]))

#Gaussian Elimination
  start_time=time.time()
  for j in range(0,n): #keep in mind we start in the 0th row and 0th column, this does j=0. j=1 and j=2
    if Aug[j,j] == 0:
      print("Matrix is not regular, try a different seed")
      break
    else:
      for i in range(j+1,n):
        Aug[i,:]=Aug[i,:]-(Aug[i,j]/Aug[j,j])*Aug[j,:]


 # display(Math(f'{latex(Aug[:,:n])}\mathbf{{x}}={latex(Aug[:,n])}'))

  endgauss_time=time.time()
  print(f"GaussianElimination took:{endgauss_time-start_time:.4f} seconds")
  sol=zeros(n,1)

#Backward Substituion
  sol[n-1]=Aug[n-1,n]/Aug[n-1,n-1] #x_n=c_n/u_{nn}
  for i in range(n-2,-1,-1):
    sol[i]=Aug[i,n]
    for j in range(i+1,n):
      sol[i]=sol[i]-(Aug[i,j]*sol[j])
    sol[i]=sol[i]/Aug[i,i]

  end_time=time.time()
  print(f"Backwards Solution took:{end_time-endgauss_time:.4f} seconds")
  print(f"Total Solution took:{end_time-start_time:.4f} seconds")
  #display(Math(f'{latex(sol)}'))
  #print("Check")
  #display(Math(f'{latex(A)}{latex(sol)}={latex(A*sol)}={latex(b)}'))







interact(f,
         seednum=widgets.IntText(value=1234, description='Seed='),
         n=widgets.IntText(value=200, description='Number of Variables='),
          );

from numpy.random import seed, choice, shuffle
from numpy import sum
from sympy import Matrix, latex, solve, zeros
from sympy.abc import x, y, z
from IPython.display import display, Math
from ipywidgets import interact
import ipywidgets as widgets
import time

def f(seednum,n):

  P=range(1,10)
  seed(seednum)
  Aug = Matrix(choice(P, (n, n+1)))


  A, b = Aug[:, :n], Aug[:, n]


  #display(Math(f'{latex(Aug[:,:n])}\mathbf{{x}}={latex(Aug[:,n])}'))
  #sol = Matrix([[x], [y], [z]])
  #display(Math(f'{latex(A)}{latex(sol)}={latex(b)}'))
  #print(solve(A*sol - b, [x, y, z]))

#Gaussian Elimination
  start_time=time.time()
  for j in range(0,n): #keep in mind we start in the 0th row and 0th column, this does j=0. j=1 and j=2
    if Aug[j,j] == 0:
      print("Matrix is not regular, try a different seed")
      break
    else:
      Aug[j,:]=Aug[j,:]/Aug[j,j]
      for i in range(j+1,n):
        Aug[i,:]=Aug[i,:]-(Aug[i,j])*Aug[j,:]

  for j in range(n-1,0,-1):
    for i in range(0,j):
      Aug[i,:]=Aug[i,:]-(Aug[i,j])*Aug[j,:]


  #display(Math(f'{latex(Aug[:,:n])}\mathbf{{x}}={latex(Aug[:,n])}'))

  endgauss_time=time.time()
  print(f"GaussianElimination took:{endgauss_time-start_time:.4f} seconds")
  sol=zeros(n,1)

#Backward Substituion
  sol=Aug[:,n]


  end_time=time.time()
  print(f"Backwards Solution took:{end_time-endgauss_time:.4f} seconds")
  print(f"Total Solution took:{end_time-start_time:.4f} seconds")
  #display(Math(f'{latex(sol)}'))
  #print("Check")
  #display(Math(f'{latex(A)}{latex(sol)}={latex(A*sol)}={latex(b)}'))
